title: The Supreme Court’s Cisco Ruling Clears the Way for U.S. Tech to Aid Repression Abroad | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: The Supreme Court's ruling for Cisco Systems in Cisco Systems, Inc. v. Doe I effectively guts the Alien Tort Statute, foreclosing corporate liability for U.S. tech firms that build surveillance tools used by repressive foreign governments (here, China's 'Golden Shield' system used against Falun Gong practitioners) — shifting future accountability efforts toward state and foreign courts.

themes.theme: The Cisco ruling and the Golden Shield record

insights.claim: The U.S. Supreme Court ruled on June 23, 2026 in favor of Cisco Systems in Cisco Systems, Inc., et al. v. Doe I, et al., denying the possibility under law of corporate liability for aiding and abetting foreign human-rights abuses, without ever examining the evidence of Cisco's alleged complicity in Chinese Communist Party repression of Falun Gong practitioners.
insights.speaker: David J. Scheffer
insights.so_what: Establishes a binding precedent that U.S. tech firms face no federal civil liability for enabling foreign-government repression, materially de-risking overseas surveillance-technology sales for tech multinationals.
insights.locator: On June 23, the U.S. Supreme Court ruled

insights.claim: Cisco developed the 'Golden Shield' surveillance software at its California offices and sold it to Chinese authorities, who allegedly used Golden Shield-derived data to identify, arrest, and torture thousands of Falun Gong practitioners, including referencing the data during beatings with an electric baton and ice-water torture sessions.
insights.speaker: David J. Scheffer
insights.so_what: Shows the direct product-to-harm evidentiary chain plaintiffs alleged — a record that will now never be tested in U.S. courts, illustrating the scale of conduct the ruling immunizes.
insights.quote: Officers referred to Golden Shield-derived information during sessions where they beat Doe I with an electric baton, leaving her bloody and swollen.
insights.locator: Respondents or their associates were each identified, apprehended, and tortured

insights.claim: The six-justice Cisco majority narrowed the 1789 Alien Tort Statute to only the 'Blackstone three' violations (safe conduct, ambassadors' rights, piracy), reversing the functional expansion the Court had established in Sosa v. Alvarez-Machain (2004) without explicitly overturning that precedent.
insights.speaker: David J. Scheffer
insights.so_what: Converts the ATS into a near-dead-letter statute, removing a structural tail-risk line item for multinationals operating in authoritarian markets — material for legal-exposure models on tech and industrial firms.
insights.quote: Today, we close the door that Sosa cracked and hold that courts may not create new causes of action for violations of international norms.
insights.locator: While the Cisco majority did not explicitly overturn Sosa

insights.claim: The majority also denied corporate and individual aiding-and-abetting liability for torture or extrajudicial killing under the Torture Victim Protection Act of 1991, closing the second liability channel the Falun Gong plaintiffs had invoked.
insights.speaker: David J. Scheffer
insights.so_what: Confirms the immunity is broad, not limited to the ATS — both major federal civil-liability channels for corporate complicity in atrocities are now closed.
insights.locator: Importantly, the majority further denied any corporate

insights.claim: By 2022, only 300 Alien Tort Statute suits had ever been filed in federal courts, with just 52 succeeding and 248 dismissed, indicating ATS liability was already a narrow, low-probability legal risk before Cisco further gutted it.
insights.speaker: David J. Scheffer
insights.so_what: Frames Cisco as converting an already-rare tail risk into a near-zero one — useful base rate for weighing how much incremental de-risking the ruling actually delivers to exposed firms.

insights.claim: Justice Sonia Sotomayor's dissent, joined mostly by Justices Elena Kagan and Ketanji Brown Jackson, argued the majority effectively overruled Sosa 'without even acknowledging that it is doing so,' calling the decision 'yet another low point in this Court's esteem for its precedents.'
insights.speaker: David J. Scheffer
insights.so_what: A three-justice dissent signals a doctrinal fault line that could shift with future Court composition or spur congressional action — worth monitoring for legislative attempts to restore ATS liability.
insights.quote: The Court…closes the courthouse doors not just to [the Falun Gong litigants], but to virtually every future litigant seeking redress for a violation of international law under the ATS.
insights.locator: In her dissent, Associate Justice Sonia Sotomayor

insights.claim: Yale law professor Harold Hongju Koh warned in Just Security that the ruling could turn the United States into a 'safe harbor for digital abusers' and implicitly signal to U.S. companies that they will not be held liable for helping any government, including the U.S. government, build mass-surveillance systems.
insights.speaker: David J. Scheffer
insights.so_what: Flags a spillover risk beyond foreign-repression cases — domestic government surveillance-tech contractors could read this ruling as emboldening, relevant to defense-tech and surveillance-tech liability exposure.
insights.quote: turn the United States into a safe harbor for digital abusers.
insights.locator: As repressive regimes around the world increasingly apply digital surveillance

insights.claim: Scheffer argues it would be unsurprising if some corporations are now incentivized to profit from violating international human-rights standards abroad, given the near-elimination of U.S. civil-liability risk.
insights.speaker: David J. Scheffer
insights.so_what: Names the moral-hazard trade directly: reduced legal downside could expand the addressable market for repression-enabling technology exports.
insights.quote: It would be unsurprising if some corporations are now incentivized to profit from violating international human rights standards abroad.
insights.locator: They rendered the ATS a toothless relic

insights.claim: With federal ATS claims largely foreclosed, Scheffer notes emerging opportunities exist to pursue multinational corporate liability claims, including aiding-and-abetting theories, in state and foreign courts instead.
insights.speaker: David J. Scheffer
insights.so_what: Signals where litigation risk migrates next — state and foreign-court venues become the relevant jurisdictional exposure to track for tech firms selling surveillance tools abroad.
insights.locator: Nonetheless, emerging opportunities exist to pursue
